result:
For most of human history the majority of breathing creatures never asked themselves why the sky was blue, because the sky, day and night, was simply a roof to their arching world. They watched it for storms and omens, for migrating birds, for the rise or fall of tides, but they did not see color as a puzzle. Color was appetite: the butchered red of prey, the bitter yellow of a leaf that warned. Blues were distance, cold, unreachable depth. I mention this to say that wonder is something we learned late, like writing; we acquired it in layers of metaphors, like coats against the ice age. And once it took root we could not leave it alone. We turned wonder over and over, polishing it until it shone, and then bored through it trying to discover whether light itself had a taste. Wonder became both consoler and saboteur: it armed us against finitude while annotating the margins of every certainty we won. A side effect is that nowadays almost any sidewalk can present itself as a miniature cosmos: a splatter of gutter water shows you stars inverted in greasy constellations; a cigarette filter wears a tiny motto printed in a gothic font you read like a relic. Every bus rides through a layered palimpsest of histories, each seat an archaeology of strangers’ sighs truncated by invisible ellipsis marks.  

I have been trying to unlearn speed, not a fashionable claim, because speed is the parent technology of modern wonder. Speed reduces the distance between appetite and satisfaction until the day is packed tight as a crate of produce bruised on its journey but still somehow glowing. The mind seduced by velocity keeps foreclosing on its own surprises, until surprise itself shrivels into plot twist instead of revelation. I used to board flights simply because I loved the lurch when wheels leave tarmac, that small sigh of impossibility. I made a ritual of counting the seconds until the seatbelt sign blinked off, a private micro-sabbath of suspension at thirty thousand feet. Yet the miracle I wanted soothed rather than startled me. It resembled comfort food: high-calorie transcendence. One winter afternoon somewhere above the Gulf of Alaska I noticed, for the first time, how the wing’s rivets scattered sparks of ice like punctuation marks. I had flown one hundred and seventy-eight times before that day, and the sparkles had always been waiting. I had blinked too quickly. Unlearning speed meant deciding to blink slowly. You cannot Instagram slow blinking. Try it: the gesture is almost obscene in public, like mouthing a secret. But the opposite of speed is reverence, and reverence is a muscle atrophied by timelines.  

The paradox is that slowness also hurts. The brain evolved in a safari where sitting still too long risked contagion. Now our bodies punish attention itself with lactic boredom, a cramp in the legs of the mind. Meditation instructions say notice the breath; I notice how immediately the breath becomes artisanal, edited, theatrical, as though the autonomic is ashamed of being mundane. Meanwhile the room hums with phantom notifications. To counteract this I go to the nearest library—not the grand whispering neoclassical kind but a fluorescent cube beside a strip mall—with the intention of reading something I do not yet want to read. The project must be useless. On a shelf of donated encyclopedic miscellanea I found, wedged between Boas and Bourdieu, a slim spiral-bound notebook labeled “Logs of a Roofing Consultant” dated 1981. A roofing consultant—someone who drives inspections of entropy, diagnosing shingles like a pathologist of domestic climate. The consultant frets about granule loss, mildew mapping, the treacherous friendliness of pine needles. The prose toggles between brusque and lyrical, the way engineers sometimes recite jargon as though quoting koans: “flashing insufficient at valley,” followed by “cloud reflection dimples the membrane like harbor water.” Between entries were stapled Polaroids of crumbling skylights with hash marks drawn directly on the emulsion—notations that felt closer to prehistoric cave art than to OSHA. I sat for four hours, a geological minute, memorizing the sheen of polyurethane as the consultant had, bathed in fluorescence that made my pupils contract to skeptical pinholes. The logs never became important; they simply began to matter, a distinction that matters. They held me, a fish speared on a line of reportage that had outlived its urgency yet retained its pulse.  

Footnote: I refuse nostalgia as stubbornly as I refuse futurism. Nostalgia gut-renovates the house of memory so that past interiors become open-plan and stylish. Futurism strips the future into a showroom highway. Both insult the resistance of reality to either mortgage. Instead I prefer maintenance: fixing gutters of the imagination so the past can drip through unimpeded, the future collect in barrels. Maintenance is much less sexy than revolution, which is probably why the earth still revolves.  

A month into my roof log dalliance, my neighbor across the alley dragged a red plastic sled into his backyard. It was April, but snow had surprised us overnight, wet heavy stuff that bent lilacs into question marks. The sled was faded like a playground relic, the kind of toy that seems always to have belonged to kids already grown beyond embarrassment. I watched, unseen, because curiosity is a form of etiquette among strangers, as he upended the sled onto sawhorses and began sawing it lengthwise. The plastic protested like a seagull until it cracked open hollow. Inside, stored for a decade or more, lay a nest of purple crocus bulbs, desiccated but strangely stately, their papery skins the color of twilight on bruised apples. He set them individually on a tray as though interviewing them. Each bulb looked like a planet shrunken to a skull. He told them (yes, aloud) that everything ends, but endings may translate into gardens. I did not know gardeners spoke in declarations strong enough to wreck me. When he finished arranging the bulbs he reached into the severed sled and removed a children’s book with a golden spine: The Little Engine That Could. Taped to the inner back cover was a folded square of brown paper in my neighbor’s boyhood handwriting: “Seeds for when I have a daughter.”  

No daughter appeared. Instead there were rows of crocuses the next week, breaking the soil exactly where the sled had been killed. Ottilie, the neighbor’s rescue greyhound, spent hours nosing the green blades, as if ashamed of her lack of chlorophyll. People are flora to dogs, reduced to scent, but flora return the favor by reminding us we are temporary topiary.  

Saturday the hardware store smelled of pine sap and guilt. I’d gone ostensibly to price caulk, yet spent twenty unidentified minutes in the rope aisle imagining escapes. Hemp was scratchy romance, paracord uncharismatic competence. The shop’s radio coughed up Fleetwood Mac, “Dreams,” the soft silver whistle of Nicks dissolving time. A toddler wearing dinosaur galoshes tottered along, mouth half open the way children listen to music before language attaches meanings. His father followed at the pace of treacle, eyes dulled by exhaustion, but when the chorus peaked something in his shoulders prised loose; he began nodding in quarter time, as though to say yes, fine, we nearly lost this song too. The child leaned backward unexpectedly, spine curving boneless wonder, trusting the dad’s palms to arch through the air like a gymnast exploding into applause. I felt the invasive tenderness that strangers sometimes witness and pretend they haven’t. Eventually the father set the boy down, and they resumed their errand, the moment fading to background. I bought paracond because my radiator pipes merely needed competence, not romance.  

Someone once demonstrated to me that an entire poem can be jury-rigged out of UPC numbers. A barcode begins and ends with 101; inside the zeroes and ones arrange themselves into stripes like Morse spoken by a zebra. If you squint at the quiet valleys between bars, they resemble miniature Russian nesting dolls of recursive identity. The cashier handed me change shaped exactly like destiny: two pennies minted different years glued by oxidation, edge to edge. I pocketed them without thinking, tumbling them into a pocket already littered with other glacial capsules: a bead off a costume necklace that belonged to a woman who left town mid-sentence; a flattened bottle cap from a brew I hated but somehow finish occasionally at 3 a.m. when sleep ghosts me. Junk archaeology. The weight of these objects has become a private gravity tugging my coat hem toward memories I did not invite.  

Sometimes I picture the city as a massive typewriter ribbon. Every footstep, every violently idling truck, every quarrel under streetlamp: the ribbon absorbs ink until it is black as the lowermost trench of atmosphere. At dawn the ribbon resets, invisible to passers-by, prepared for a new martyrdom, but microscopic flakes of ink cling to curbstones, to boot treads, traceable in municipal DNA tests of decades. Once I met a woman who scraped bits of curb gunk at 2:15 a.m. with a palette knife she stole from her failed art degree, then melted the gunk onto handmade paper. She called the results travelogues. When pressed to name a destination, she said Home.  

I have spent equal parts of my life trying to leave and trying to return, often within a single afternoon. There is an hour after lunch when light leans at such an angle that every window breather is granted a cameo of their own disappearances. You pause in the doorway holding coffee grown cold, and the doorframe becomes a gallows without malice. Your shadow stretches east; somewhere someone else’s reaches west; if both shadows ever shook hands, the sun would go out.  

One Wednesday I met up with an older sibling I rarely see because geography keeps siding with grudges. We agreed to meet at a theater whose marquee letters had migrated south—only the vowels remained, spelling OEOIA, like a yawn translated into Latin. A matinee of a 1947 film neither of us had ever watched, The Ghost and Mrs. Muir. Inside, a smear of usher with proper 1950s pomade showed us seats beneath a balcony hung with ceramic chandeliers like quantities of frozen sherbet. The sound crackled, a tactile patina over dialogue. The screen itself had stains that turned stars into punctuation. In one scene the sea captain ghost dictates his salty memoir to a skeptical widow; the captain describes night waves as “thick enough to keelhaul the moon.” The line scraped through the cheap speakers and lodged into both of us simultaneously—we heard the same words, split the same sigh among siblings who once choreographed elaborate fights over the pronunciation of mayonnaise. Memory can be discordant but wonder is harmonizing. The lights came up on our blinking faces, decades unrolled between us—and maybe it wasn’t healing, because healing is dramatic, but rather a resting, like landing a taut kettledrum hand stilled in midsong. Walking into daylight, we matched strides although we no longer shared shoe sizes. The vowels above the lobby looked less like yawning now and more like small birds taking flight.  

The body is faster at archiving loss than anything else. A scraped knee at age seven sailed into my future self as a faint moon crimson beside the bathtub whenever water ran hot. Hunger also remains portable, even when metabolically unnecessary. The smell of stale communion wafers can melt a dignified adult into some reverberating pew creature who believes cranberry juice is literal blood. Smell is a Trojan horse wheeled directly into the hippocampus; it does not knock. Last spring my laundromat adopted a detergent so floral it outlawed other perfumes. Every dryer cycle made patrons cry without consent; dryers are emotional ovens. One woman used to fold diabetic socks with surgical precision, praised the detergent as “bridal,” then confessed her son had married behind her back in a courthouse, mailed invitations written on tissue paper. She folded and unfolded the birth announcement like a score she feared to get wrong. The dryers kept sighing hot artificial peonies; outside real magnolias canceled winter overnight, their scent subtler than detergent and unaffordable by memory.  

Curiosity is a variant of appetite that never quite reaches fullness. Which might explain why I once found myself studying moss the way art historians study interior perspective. Moss is not green; it is a symposium on green. Roll a rotten log and discover entire empires of itinerant emerald reefs. One group of gametophytes shimmer metallic, others matte like chalk study shadows. Their veins are scalpels of moisture. See the tiny sporophytes lifting capsules they will never cradle again, catapults tasked with both beginnings and farewells. When you look away your finger retains the fungal smell of something already composting forward to next spring’s skunk cabbage, a reminder that decay leases luxury tastes to the living.  

There’s a lighthouse thirty miles north that hasn’t had a keeper since 1987. The mechanism is automatic, solar dependent; the Coast Guard visits twice a year with a clipboard and indifference. Tourists still infest the cliff path in season wielding selfie sticks like aerial roots. The single structure not posted no-trespass is the shed where legendary oilskins hang nailed to cedar walls, blackened raincoats stiffened as the men who wore them. Their buttons are bakelite constellations. If you touch nothing the air mutters diesel and salt, currencies of vigilance. I visited once in November when storms stacked like dinner plates. No employees, just wind and an empty suggestion box bolted to the fence reading “Improvements welcome.” Inside the slack mouth lay damp envelopes addressed Dear Lighthouse or To Whom It Foamy Concern. A child wrote in crayon that the lighthouse should add a slide; a divorcé asked that the beam announce the exact moment forgiveness is impossible; a retiree enclosed a grocery coupon and apologized for being rude in 1962. What becomes of unmailed prayers? I pocketed one blank envelope stamped with the phrase Post No Illegible. Maybe improbable futures can be smuggled under ordinary postage. Maybe the lighthouse sorts them at night while the beam erases itself by passing, like a mother checking doors without remembering why.  

I am perpetually reaching the age when every ornamental detail of childhood suddenly reasserts its narrative rights. Saturday morning cartoons emerge from archives as documentaries watching us watching. One Saturday in reality I woke at 6 a.m. to test if the burn scar on my forearm still remembered the electric kettle incident from 1998. The scar did, with a plum heat that migrated elsewhere. I made coffee and found online the full episode of “The Real Ghostbusters” where Egon is possessed by a singing hat. I remembered the hat was purple, but the restored footage showed it magenta flickering to ultraviolet, colors no cathode-ray tube could render in the 20th century. Technology recolors our amnesia. Slimer used to scare me; now I sympathize with him as green transparent hunger. My nephew age six recently asked why ghosts in cartoons live in haunted houses when houses themselves are dead lumber. I explained that maybe ghosts think they are homesick in reverse, and he nodded solemnly, which is how children tip adults into humility. Later he drew a picture: a ghost hugging a floorplan. The house is weeping windows.  

Some distances are not traversable by foot. When my friend Evan underwent chemotherapy the last survivors of his eyebrows resembled delicate diacritical marks above eyes that had become parentheses. We played chess on a miniature magnetic set intended for campers. Pieces adhered despite tremor. After each game he incrementally removed ranks like teeth, finishing in hollow endgames. One afternoon he said he wanted to visit Stonehenge at dawn. We both knew travel bans, coughs rusted red. Instead I stole a Styrofoam cooler, spray-painted it grey, and glued symmetrical stones made of bath bombs. He laughed carefully, not to dislodge ports. Arrayed on the windowsill our model henge fizzed in spring sunlight emitting a menthol aroma that evacuated the antiseptic staleness of the ward. When dissolving, citric galaxies swirled that looked identical to PET scan clouds they’d shown us the week prior. The cooler drained turquoise onto the sill paint, evidence of ritual leak. A nurse scolded about mold risks. Evan died in November. The stain persists like a bruise on the window-side where weather is pacing. Whenever I see Stonehenge calendars sold in airport kiosks, I imagine travelers buying miniature cores of illness to gift relatives.  

Words, too, have cancers. The word “actually” metastasizes statements into corrections. The word “literally” flat-irons metaphor into brittle timidity. Evan and I used to text each other entire conversations using only auto-predict completions until wisdom broke down into giggling. He sent: “The weather today incontinence forest fire.” I replied: “Please bring me the other side of the planet.” The recursive abyss of algorithmic empathy. Sometimes a wrong word saves us temporarily from the right sorrow.  

There are people who devote entire gardens to plants that bloom at night, heliophobes like evening primrose, moonflowers, nicotiana, angels who refuse evangelism. Last July I visited one such ritual plot belonging to a city rooftop rented by three sisters named Quietude, Solace, and Story. They had tattooed asterisks at the base of their thumbs to signal blood type in emergencies. The moonflowers, pale trumpets unfurling at dusk, smelled like laundry forgotten on the line by a widower. Solace cut one bloom and held it under a flashlight to prove to me the translucence hides veins blue as subway maps. A bee once returned convinced it was dawn; they guided it back to the edge of rooftop like docents returning a sleepwalker. Story cooked on a camp stove lentils flavored with cardamom and rainwater. We ate cross-legged under a tarp while taxi meters blinked code into the city veins twenty floors below. Quietude produces ambient music from recordings of chalk on blackboard; her phone contained files labeled Chorus of Hesitation, Sonata for Unanswered Texts. She played us a composition synthesized from rooftop raindrop gradients, the sound reverb drenched like confessionals. Around 2 a.m. a misfired firework from someone’s engagement party arced up directly opposite the moon. The explosion embroidered temporary lace across the indigo. No one commented verbally; we simply inhaled synchronously the cordite and tuberose, an accidental incense. After the sisters slept, I walked the perimeter noting that rows of nicotiana leaned outward like aristocrats flirting with balcony leaps. In the distance the financial district cubicles stayed lit as though insomnia were a union requirement. One entire building façade displayed green LED circuitry like a giant motherboard grimacing at its own mortality. I remained until the moon surrendered to glowless surrender; the moonflowers by then had collapsed shut like small fists keeping secrets.  

Morning diffused into the absurdity of coffee chains. I ordered a medium roast labelled Guatemalan Dark Roast Farmer Loveseat—as though couches cultivate beans—and received a paper cup quoting Rumi: “The wound is the place where the light enters you.” Corporate spirituality tastes burnt. A stranger beside me spilled milk onto the counter into a pattern resembling Pangaea. I apologized reflexively; he looked surprised, claimed ownership of the spill. We briefly co-authored its topography. He pushed two continents together, creating an isthmus of 2% lactose. A barista wiped us clean into geography as though unconvinced by revisionist earth.  

I want a taxonomy for these moments that exist beyond politics, below commodity—a Linnaean system for Glancing Up at the Exact Minor Chord Change in Supermarket Music. Or for the miniature courtroom enacted every time two dogs meet nose to tail. The charm of this taxonomy would require dysfunction: mislabelled drawers, Latin corrupted by autocorrect. Imagine a field guide entry: “Behavior #417: Humans pretending not to see other humans cry on public transport. Habitat: commuter hours. Threat Level: negligible, though chronic exposure erodes spines.”  

Back home there was suddenly too much sky inside internet windows. I closed them and walked west until pavement ran out at a boat launch reverting to marsh. I counted fifteen pylons smeared algae green. The water was a tutorial on reflection: how trees practicing nonexistence still approximated identity. I borrowed a quote accidentally from Diane Arbus: “A photograph is a secret about a secret.” Here, the river photographed nothing but kept secreting moonlight. At dusk I watched an elderly man attempt to remove a motorized wheelchair from a sedan trunk while managing a dog leash in the same hand. The dog, ancient golden retriever, stood with the dignity of a retired oracle. Every time the man’s grip slipped, the chair rolled precarious inches toward the ramp as though tempted by aquatic retirement. I offered help; the man waved me off gently. He wanted ownership. Eventually he threaded physics and longing into a successful descent. The chair touched gravel, dog wheezed approval. Both gathered momentum. He paused to pet the dog in a language no translator could corrupt: thumbs around ears, faint whistle, promise. Then he revved the motor, dog trotting crookedly beside, and they disappeared down the launch like pilgrims entering a medieval woodcut. The river accepted them: silver wrinkles briefly embroidered their bodies, then healed seamless. I stood holding nothing as geese overhead V-ed toward a future neither linear nor fated.  

Returning, I passed an abandoned elementary school. Drawn by a broken window, I entered cautiously, flashlight unnecessary: western auroras of sunset painted everything coral. Inside, desks arranged like biscuit before the fire drill of time. Chalkboard equations had been frozen mid-eruption, spirals of decimal points resembling snail trails. On one floor, a clutch of wilting valentines from February 2004 waited in a cardboard mailbox fashioned from a tissue box. Names still legible: Marcus hearts Alison, emoji absent, the word forever spelled correctly. I could not bring myself to steal evidence. Instead I chose a crayon stub labeled Asparagus and added single glyph: a tilde ~ symbol implying undulation, continuity, maybe apology. Years later a child may decipher it as caterpillar, or wave, or ripple in existence left by a adult momentarily remembering homework.  

Consider that hummingbirds shoulder more weight than internal combustion engines relative to muscle mass, yet fear stops them from wearing seatbelts. Consider that the word shoulder used to be spelled schulder until printers misread long s as f ensuring generations believed arms were foldlers. All language is a sustained typo against silence.  

In bed later I listened through thin walls to neighbor practicing cello at 11:47 p.m. He resisted the melody until it yielded, a minor key resignation. Music occupies space physically; that night gravity rearranged so that sound pooled under my tongue. I tasted resin and strings. Through the window leaked streetlight amber, the color of pages in antique Bibles, the hue my grandmother swore would keep nightmares from eating her feet. Amber: sap trapped insects in prehistoric June; now trapping my room in hemisphere of Saturday at an impossible volume. Cello invoked phrase in me: I am saving a seat for your returns. But from where? Death? Elementary school? The palette knife samaritan whose Stonehenge bath bombs melted? The woman who scraped curbs for forgetfulness? I fell asleep mid-note; the bow kept sawing through my dream insisting destination exists inside sustain.  

Sunday brought municipal elections with turnout low enough volunteers hand-wrapped ballots like artisan chocolate. Voting booth curtains smelled of mothballs and quiet shouting. I filled ovals deliberately, refusing the stylus that looked like Q-tip of bureaucracy, preferring graphite’s molecular diffusion into paper; history needs pores. Afterward I bought a bagel and watched democracy tally itself on a library scrolling screen—numbers climbed but joy lagged. Someone dropped pizza on sidewalk instantaneously orphaned, a small circle of childhood abandonment. Pigeons convened. Politics continued elsewhere on phones, but birds voted unanimously with wings.  

That night I located the envelope labeled “Post No Illegible,” unsigned but addressed to “Return When Sure.” I added my own sentence to the accidental ledger inside: “I have learned to measure time not in days or hours but in the interval between someone choosing to sing and someone choosing to listen.” The sentence felt cheesy until I sealed the envelope, then it felt sacred. I mailed it c/o The Lighthouse, dropped it blue as a vein into a postbox that might redirect to compost. Somewhere possibly an automated beam will open my admission and scan it for fear levels exceeding legibility.  

Downstairs the lobby fluorescents flickered the Morse of insomnia. An elderly woman paced with a walker while reciting multiplication tables. Sixty-four sevens equal four hundred forty-eight. Her uniform: coral mohair cardigan, navy skirt, the kind of outfit people call timeless but is actually specific to an age terrified of color. I asked if she needed anything; she replied she was finishing a report due in 1955. Her teacher had assigned perpetual homework. I imagined whole archives of children still transmitting overdue fractions into ether, and she merely among the last still receiving mail. I sat down without invitation on the plastic bench meant for packages. Together we recited eight times eight until an orderly collected her into the elevator like a misplaced chapter.  

Maybe there is no cumulative paragraph in which I wrap these glaciations of observation into thesis. The thesis may be that there is no thesis. Only moments reenacting tides: they withdraw, they drag sand into foreign beds, they return with fresh wreckage beautiful beyond utility. I write not to uncover metaphysics but to practice shelving astonishment where I might re-encounter before forgetfulness cremates it. It is enough that I remember the elderly man lowering his wheelchair toward dark water, the bee misreading moon, the crocus bulbs inside sled womb, the loan of an entire ocean preserved by a woman who thought her son watched from shore. All of it blossoms at irregular intervals, like those night gardens that refuse longitude. One day I will be archived unmailed, a voice roaming some hallway of a building not yet built, reciting tilde wormholes left on chalkboards, hoping a late insomniac transcribes me into valentine for no season in particular. Until then I keep listening, because listening is the only technology faster than light.

Junk archaeology. The weight of these objects has become a private gravity tugging my coat hem toward memories I did not invite.  

Sometimes I picture the city as a massive typewriter ribbon. Every footstep, every violently idling truck, every quarrel under streetlamp: the ribbon absorbs ink until it is black as the lowermost trench of atmosphere. At dawn the ribbon resets, invisible to passers-by, prepared for a new martyrdom, but microscopic flakes of ink cling to curbstones, to boot treads, traceable in municipal DNA tests of decades. Once I met a woman who scraped bits of curb gunk at 2:15 a.m. with a palette knife she stole from her failed art degree, then melted the gunk onto handmade paper. She called the results travelogues. When pressed to name a destination, she said Home.  

I have spent equal parts of my life trying to leave and trying to return, often within a single afternoon. There is an hour after lunch when light leans at such an angle that every window breather is granted a cameo of their own disappearances. You pause in the doorway holding coffee grown cold, and the doorframe becomes a gallows without malice. Your shadow stretches east; somewhere someone else’s reaches west; if both shadows ever shook hands, the sun would go out.  

One Wednesday I met up with an older sibling I rarely see because geography keeps siding with grudges. We agreed to meet at a theater whose marquee letters had migrated south—only the vowels remained, spelling OEOIA, like a yawn translated into Latin. A matinee of a 1947 film neither of us had ever watched, The Ghost and Mrs. Muir. Inside, a smear of usher with proper 1950s pomade showed us seats beneath a balcony hung with ceramic chandeliers like quantities of frozen sherbet. The sound crackled, a tactile patina over dialogue. The screen itself had stains that turned stars into punctuation. In one scene the sea captain ghost dictates his salty memoir to a skeptical widow; the captain describes night waves as “thick enough to keelhaul the moon.” The line scraped through the cheap speakers and lodged into both of us simultaneously—we heard the same words, split the same sigh among siblings who once choreographed elaborate fights over the pronunciation of mayonnaise. Memory can be discordant but wonder is harmonizing. The lights came up on our blinking faces, decades unrolled between us—and maybe it wasn’t healing, because healing is dramatic, but rather a resting, like landing a taut kettledrum hand stilled in midsong. Walking into daylight, we matched strides although we no longer shared shoe sizes. The vowels above the lobby looked less like yawning now and more like small birds taking flight.  

The body is faster at archiving loss than anything else. A scraped knee at age seven sailed into my future self as a faint moon crimson beside the bathtub whenever water ran hot. Hunger also remains portable, even when metabolically unnecessary. The smell of stale communion wafers can melt a dignified adult into some reverberating pew creature who believes cranberry juice is literal blood. Smell is a Trojan horse wheeled directly into the hippocampus; it does not knock. Last spring my laundromat adopted a detergent so floral it outlawed other perfumes. Every dryer cycle made patrons cry without consent; dryers are emotional ovens. One woman used to fold diabetic socks with surgical precision, praised the detergent as “bridal,” then confessed her son had married behind her back in a courthouse, mailed invitations written on tissue paper. She folded and unfolded the birth announcement like a score she feared to get wrong. The dryers kept sighing hot artificial peonies; outside real magnolias canceled winter overnight, their scent subtler than detergent and unaffordable by memory.  

Curiosity is a variant of appetite that never quite reaches fullness. Which might explain why I once found myself studying moss the way art historians study interior perspective. Moss is not green; it is a symposium on green. Roll a rotten log and discover entire empires of itinerant emerald reefs. One group of gametophytes shimmer metallic, others matte like chalk study shadows. Their veins are scalpels of moisture. See the tiny sporophytes lifting capsules they will never cradle again, catapults tasked with both beginnings and farewells. When you look away your finger retains the fungal smell of something already composting forward to next spring’s skunk cabbage, a reminder that decay leases luxury tastes to the living.  

There’s a lighthouse thirty miles north that hasn’t had a keeper since 1987. The mechanism is automatic, solar dependent; the Coast Guard visits twice a year with a clipboard and indifference. Tourists still infest the cliff path in season wielding selfie sticks like aerial roots. The single structure not posted no-trespass is the shed where legendary oilskins hang nailed to cedar walls, blackened raincoats stiffened as the men who wore them. Their buttons are bakelite constellations. If you touch nothing the air mutters diesel and salt, currencies of vigilance. I visited once in November when storms stacked like dinner plates. No employees, just wind and an empty suggestion box bolted to the fence reading “Improvements welcome.” Inside the slack mouth lay damp envelopes addressed Dear Lighthouse or To Whom It Foamy Concern. A child wrote in crayon that the lighthouse should add a slide; a divorcé asked that the beam announce the exact moment forgiveness is impossible; a retiree enclosed a grocery coupon and apologized for being rude in 1962. What becomes of unmailed prayers? I pocketed one blank envelope stamped with the phrase Post No Illegible. Maybe improbable futures can be smuggled under ordinary postage. Maybe the lighthouse sorts them at night while the beam erases itself by passing, like a mother checking doors without remembering why.  

I am perpetually reaching the age when every ornamental detail of childhood suddenly reasserts its narrative rights. Saturday morning cartoons emerge from archives as documentaries watching us watching. One Saturday in reality I woke at 6 a.m. to test if the burn scar on my forearm still remembered the electric kettle incident from 1998. The scar did, with a plum heat that migrated elsewhere. I made coffee and found online the full episode of “The Real Ghostbusters” where Egon is possessed by a singing hat. I remembered the hat was purple, but the restored footage showed it magenta flickering to ultraviolet, colors no cathode-ray tube could render in the 20th century. Technology recolors our amnesia. Slimer used to scare me; now I sympathize with him as green transparent hunger. My nephew age six recently asked why ghosts in cartoons live in haunted houses when houses themselves are dead lumber. I explained that maybe ghosts think they are homesick in reverse, and he nodded solemnly, which is how children tip adults into humility. Later he drew a picture: a ghost hugging a floorplan. The house is weeping windows.  

Some distances are not traversable by foot. When my friend Evan underwent chemotherapy the last survivors of his eyebrows resembled delicate diacritical marks above eyes that had become parentheses. We played chess on a miniature magnetic set intended for campers. Pieces adhered despite tremor. After each game he incrementally removed ranks like teeth, finishing in hollow endgames. One afternoon he said he wanted to visit Stonehenge at dawn. We both knew travel bans, coughs rusted red. Instead I stole a Styrofoam cooler, spray-painted it grey, and glued symmetrical stones made of bath bombs. He laughed carefully, not to dislodge ports. Arrayed on the windowsill our model henge fizzed in spring sunlight emitting a menthol aroma that evacuated the antiseptic staleness of the ward. When dissolving, citric galaxies swirled that looked identical to PET scan clouds they’d shown us the week prior. The cooler drained turquoise onto the sill paint, evidence of ritual leak. A nurse scolded about mold risks. Evan died in November. The stain persists like a bruise on the window-side where weather is pacing. Whenever I see Stonehenge calendars sold in airport kiosks, I imagine travelers buying miniature cores of illness to gift relatives.  

Words, too, have cancers. The word “actually” metastasizes statements into corrections. The word “literally” flat-irons metaphor into brittle timidity. Evan and I used to text each other entire conversations using only auto-predict completions until wisdom broke down into giggling. He sent: “The weather today incontinence forest fire.” I replied: “Please bring me the other side of the planet.” The recursive abyss of algorithmic empathy. Sometimes a wrong word saves us temporarily from the right sorrow.  

There are people who devote entire gardens to plants that bloom at night, heliophobes like evening primrose, moonflowers, nicotiana, angels who refuse evangelism. Last July I visited one such ritual plot belonging to a city rooftop rented by three sisters named Quietude, Solace, and Story. They had tattooed asterisks at the base of their thumbs to signal blood type in emergencies. The moonflowers, pale trumpets unfurling at dusk, smelled like laundry forgotten on the line by a widower. Solace cut one bloom and held it under a flashlight to prove to me the translucence hides veins blue as subway maps. A bee once returned convinced it was dawn; they guided it back to the edge of rooftop like docents returning a sleepwalker. Story cooked on a camp stove lentils flavored with cardamom and rainwater. We ate cross-legged under a tarp while taxi meters blinked code into the city veins twenty floors below. Quietude produces ambient music from recordings of chalk on blackboard; her phone contained files labeled Chorus of Hesitation, Sonata for Unanswered Texts. She played us a composition synthesized from rooftop raindrop gradients, the sound reverb drenched like confessionals. Around 2 a.m. a misfired firework from someone’s engagement party arced up directly opposite the moon. The explosion embroidered temporary lace across the indigo. No one commented verbally; we simply inhaled synchronously the cordite and tuberose, an accidental incense. After the sisters slept, I walked the perimeter noting that rows of nicotiana leaned outward like aristocrats flirting with balcony leaps. In the distance the financial district cubicles stayed lit as though insomnia were a union requirement. One entire building façade displayed green LED circuitry like a giant motherboard grimacing at its own mortality. I remained until the moon surrendered to glowless surrender; the moonflowers by then had collapsed shut like small fists keeping secrets.  

Morning diffused into the absurdity of coffee chains. I ordered a medium roast labelled Guatemalan Dark Roast Farmer Loveseat—as though couches cultivate beans—and received a paper cup quoting Rumi: “The wound is the place where the light enters you.” Corporate spirituality tastes burnt. A stranger beside me spilled milk onto the counter into a pattern resembling Pangaea. I apologized reflexively; he looked surprised, claimed ownership of the spill. We briefly co-authored its topography. He pushed two continents together, creating an isthmus of 2% lactose. A barista wiped us clean into geography as though unconvinced by revisionist earth.  

I want a taxonomy for these moments that exist beyond politics, below commodity—a Linnaean system for Glancing Up at the Exact Minor Chord Change in Supermarket Music. Or for the miniature courtroom enacted every time two dogs meet nose to tail. The charm of this taxonomy would require dysfunction: mislabelled drawers, Latin corrupted by autocorrect. Imagine a field guide entry: “Behavior #417: Humans pretending not to see other humans cry on public transport. Habitat: commuter hours. Threat Level: negligible, though chronic exposure erodes spines.”  

Back home there was suddenly too much sky inside internet windows. I closed them and walked west until pavement ran out at a boat launch reverting to marsh. I counted fifteen pylons smeared algae green. The water was a tutorial on reflection: how trees practicing nonexistence still approximated identity. I borrowed a quote accidentally from Diane Arbus: “A photograph is a secret about a secret.” Here, the river photographed nothing but kept secreting moonlight. At dusk I watched an elderly man attempt to remove a motorized wheelchair from a sedan trunk while managing a dog leash in the same hand. The dog, ancient golden retriever, stood with the dignity of a retired oracle. Every time the man’s grip slipped, the chair rolled precarious inches toward the ramp as though tempted by aquatic retirement. I offered help; the man waved me off gently. He wanted ownership. Eventually he threaded physics and longing into a successful descent. The chair touched gravel, dog wheezed approval. Both gathered momentum. He paused to pet the dog in a language no translator could corrupt: thumbs around ears, faint whistle, promise. Then he revved the motor, dog trotting crookedly beside, and they disappeared down the launch like pilgrims entering a medieval woodcut. The river accepted them: silver wrinkles briefly embroidered their bodies, then healed seamless. I stood holding nothing as geese overhead V-ed toward a future neither linear nor fated.  

Returning, I passed an abandoned elementary school. Drawn by a broken window, I entered cautiously, flashlight unnecessary: western auroras of sunset painted everything coral. Inside, desks arranged like biscuit before the fire drill of time. Chalkboard equations had been frozen mid-eruption, spirals of decimal points resembling snail trails. On one floor, a clutch of wilting valentines from February 2004 waited in a cardboard mailbox fashioned from a tissue box. Names still legible: Marcus hearts Alison, emoji absent, the word forever spelled correctly. I could not bring myself to steal evidence. Instead I chose a crayon stub labeled Asparagus and added single glyph: a tilde ~ symbol implying undulation, continuity, maybe apology. Years later a child may decipher it as caterpillar, or wave, or ripple in existence left by a adult momentarily remembering homework.  

Consider that hummingbirds shoulder more weight than internal combustion engines relative to muscle mass, yet fear stops them from wearing seatbelts. Consider that the word shoulder used to be spelled schulder until printers misread long s as f ensuring generations believed arms were foldlers. All language is a sustained typo against silence.  

In bed later I listened through thin walls to neighbor practicing cello at 11:47 p.m. He resisted the melody until it yielded, a minor key resignation. Music occupies space physically; that night gravity rearranged so that sound pooled under my tongue. I tasted resin and strings. Through the window leaked streetlight amber, the color of pages in antique Bibles, the hue my grandmother swore would keep nightmares from eating her feet. Amber: sap trapped insects in prehistoric June; now trapping my room in hemisphere of Saturday at an impossible volume. Cello invoked phrase in me: I am saving a seat for your returns. But from where? Death? Elementary school? The palette knife samaritan whose Stonehenge bath bombs melted? The woman who scraped curbs for forgetfulness? I fell asleep mid-note; the bow kept sawing through my dream insisting destination exists inside sustain.  

Sunday brought municipal elections with turnout low enough volunteers hand-wrapped ballots like artisan chocolate. Voting booth curtains smelled of mothballs and quiet shouting. I filled ovals deliberately, refusing the stylus that looked like Q-tip of bureaucracy, preferring graphite’s molecular diffusion into paper; history needs pores. Afterward I bought a bagel and watched democracy tally itself on a library scrolling screen—numbers climbed but joy lagged. Someone dropped pizza on sidewalk instantaneously orphaned, a small circle of childhood abandonment. Pigeons convened. Politics continued elsewhere on phones, but birds voted unanimously with wings.  

That night I located the envelope labeled “Post No Illegible,” unsigned but addressed to “Return When Sure.” I added my own sentence to the accidental ledger inside: “I have learned to measure time not in days or hours but in the interval between someone choosing to sing and someone choosing to listen.” The sentence felt cheesy until I sealed the envelope, then it felt sacred. I mailed it c/o The Lighthouse, dropped it blue as a vein into a postbox that might redirect to compost. Somewhere possibly an automated beam will open my admission and scan it for fear levels exceeding legibility.  

Downstairs the lobby fluorescents flickered the Morse of insomnia. An elderly woman paced with a walker while reciting multiplication tables. Sixty-four sevens equal four hundred forty-eight. Her uniform: coral mohair cardigan, navy skirt, the kind of outfit people call timeless but is actually specific to an age terrified of color. I asked if she needed anything; she replied she was finishing a report due in 1955. Her teacher had assigned perpetual homework. I imagined whole archives of children still transmitting overdue fractions into ether, and she merely among the last still receiving mail. I sat down without invitation on the plastic bench meant for packages. Together we recited eight times eight until an orderly collected her into the elevator like a misplaced chapter.  

Maybe there is no cumulative paragraph in which I wrap these glaciations of observation into thesis. The thesis may be that there is no thesis. Only moments reenacting tides: they withdraw, they drag sand into foreign beds, they return with fresh wreckage beautiful beyond utility. I write not to uncover metaphysics but to practice shelving astonishment where I might re-encounter before forgetfulness cremates it. It is enough that I remember the elderly man lowering his wheelchair toward dark water, the bee misreading moon, the crocus bulbs inside sled womb, the loan of an entire ocean preserved by a woman who thought her son watched from shore. All of it blossoms at irregular intervals, like those night gardens that refuse longitude. One day I will be archived unmailed, a voice roaming some hallway of a building not yet built, reciting tilde wormholes left on chalkboards, hoping a late insomniac transcribes me into valentine for no season in particular. Until then I keep listening, because listening is the only technology faster than light.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
